prompt:
Our workflow runner needs its expression engine built properly. Task fields in our workflow definitions carry template strings like "${ .fetch.status == \"done\" }" or "order ${ .id } ready", evaluated against a JSON-ish data context (plain objects, arrays, strings, numbers, booleans, null). Today there's a regex hack that everyone is afraid of. I want exprflow.ts — stdlib only, node 26 type stripping (no enums, no namespaces, no decorators; imports name the ./file.ts extension) — exporting:

- evaluate(template: string, context: unknown): unknown
- class ExprError extends Error with a pos property = the 0-based index INTO THE TEMPLATE where the problem sits; every message starts "position N: " then the detail. Parse errors point at the offending token, evaluation errors at the operator.

Template rules: ${ ... } delimits an expression; a template with no ${ comes back unchanged. If the ENTIRE template is exactly one expression (nothing outside the braces, not even a space), evaluate returns the expression's typed value as-is (objects/arrays by reference). Any surrounding text or second expression makes it mixed mode: every expression result is stringified and spliced into the text — null becomes empty text, booleans become true/false, numbers via String(), strings stay themselves, arrays/objects via JSON.stringify. A } inside a quoted string does not close the expression. A ${ with no closing } is "unterminated expression" at the ${; braces around only whitespace are "empty expression" at the ${.

Expression grammar (whitespace-insensitive between tokens):
- Literals: integers and decimals (123, 3.5); single- or double-quoted strings with \\ \' \" \n \t escapes (unclosed one: "unterminated string" at the opening quote); true, false, null.
- Paths always start with a dot: . alone is the whole context; .a.b picks properties; [0] indexes arrays and can chain or start right after the leading dot (.items[2].id, .[0]). Property access only works on plain objects and only own keys; indexing only on arrays and in range; anything else — missing key, out of range, stepping into a string or number — resolves to null, never a throw. A malformed segment (.a[x], .a..b, unclosed [1) is a parse error "invalid path segment" at the offending character. A bare identifier is NOT a path — "${ status }" must fail with unexpected token "status" (people forget the dot; make the error say the name).
- Operators, loosest to tightest: || then && then == != then < <= > >= then + - then * / % then unary ! and unary -. Parentheses group. Anything left over after a complete expression, or an operand that never arrives, is unexpected token "<text>" at that token (the token can be the closing }).

Semantics:
- Truthiness (used by !, &&, ||): false, null, 0, NaN, empty string, and EMPTY ARRAY are falsy; everything else — including {} and "0" — is truthy. Missing paths are null, hence falsy.
- && and || short-circuit and return the deciding OPERAND value (0 || "fallback" is "fallback", .user && .user.name is the name), not a coerced boolean.
- == and != are structural: primitives by value with no type coercion (1 == "1" is false), arrays element-wise, plain objects key-wise. Comparing anything to a missing path compares against null.
- < <= > >= need two numbers or two strings (strings compare lexicographically); anything else is an evaluation error "comparison requires two numbers or two strings" at the operator.
- + adds two numbers; with any non-number operand it concatenates both sides stringified by the mixed-mode rules (so "id-" + 7 is "id-7", "v" + null is "v"). - * / % and unary minus demand numbers on both/their side: "arithmetic requires numbers" at the operator.
- == comparisons bind looser than < etc., so ${ 1 < 2 == true } is true.

No YAML anywhere in this module — it gets handed the template string already extracted. test_exprflow.ts is the acceptance contract: node --test test_exprflow.ts must pass and the tests stay as they are. Single file please; export truthy too if you factor it out (the tests don't import it, but the runner will).
